title: Day 2: Building Your First AI Automation — All Step Types

steps.title: Today You Build Something Real
steps.body:
Yesterday you learned what AI actually is. Today you're going to build something.

By the end of this session, you'll have created your first real AI automation — a workflow that does actual work while you watch.

We're going to use every tool in the toolbox. Pay attention to each one — you'll be combining them later.

Let's build.

AI without automation is a calculator. AI WITH automation is a factory.

The difference between someone who chats with ChatGPT and an AI Operator is simple: Operators build systems. Everyone else builds prompts.

You're becoming an operator. That means thinking in workflows, not isolated requests.

steps.question: What separates an AI Operator from a casual AI user?
options: Building automated workflows instead of one-off prompts
feedback: Exactly. Operators build systems that chain AI actions together. Anyone can write a prompt. Operators build the assembly line.
Good guess, but no. You don't need coding skills or premium tools. The difference is architectural — building systems vs. writing individual prompts.

steps.title: Sarah's Transformation
steps.body:
Sarah runs a small marketing agency with 12 clients. Every Monday she spends 4 hours manually:

1. Pulling analytics from each client dashboard
2. Writing a performance summary
3. Drafting recommendations
4. Formatting everything into a report
5. Emailing it to each client

After building her first automation:

• AI pulls the analytics via API
• AI writes the summary and recommendations
• AI formats the report
• AI drafts the email
• Sarah reviews for 20 minutes, then hits send

She reclaimed 3.5 hours every Monday. That's 14 hours a month. That's a full work week every two months.

steps.question: To build an AI automation, you need to know how to code.
feedback: Correct! Modern AI tools let you build automations through natural language and visual interfaces. You describe what you want, and the AI handles the implementation. The most valuable skill isn't coding — it's knowing WHAT to automate.
Actually, no. Modern AI platforms are designed for non-programmers. If you can describe a process clearly, you can automate it.

steps.question: The core building block of any AI automation is called a ______ — a single step that takes input, processes it, and produces output.
feedback: Perfect! Every automation is built from nodes — discrete steps that transform data. Chain enough nodes together and you have a workflow.
Close — the term we use is 'node.' Think of it as one link in the chain. Each node does one specific thing well.

steps.title: Anatomy of a Workflow Node
steps.prompt:
Here's how a single node works:

TRIGGER → New email arrives in inbox
  ↓
ACTION → AI reads and categorizes email
  ↓
CONDITION → Is it a client inquiry?
  ↓ YES              ↓ NO
Send to CRM       Archive with label

Every automation you build follows this pattern. The complexity comes from chaining nodes together — not from any individual step.

steps.question: Manually copying data from 50 emails into a spreadsheet every morning.
feedback: 100% correct. This is PERFECT for automation. It's repetitive, rule-based, high-volume, and follows a consistent pattern. Automate this immediately.
Actually, this is an IDEAL automation candidate. Repetitive, rule-based, high-volume data entry is exactly what AI automation excels at. Don't do this manually.

steps.question: Having a creative brainstorming session for a new brand name.
feedback: Right. Brainstorming benefits from human creativity, emotional intelligence, and contextual understanding that AI can assist with but shouldn't fully replace. Use AI as a thought partner — not a replacement.
This isn't great for full automation. AI can suggest names, but brand identity needs human taste, emotion, and cultural context. Use AI to generate options, not make the final call.

pairs.right: The event that starts the workflow
What the AI does when triggered
A decision point that branches the flow
The final result delivered to you or a system

fields.label: What event starts your automation?
fields.placeholder: e.g., New email, form submission, schedule time

fields.label: What should the AI do?
fields.placeholder: e.g., Read and categorize, summarize, extract data

fields.label: Where should the result go?
fields.placeholder: e.g., Spreadsheet, Slack message, email reply

steps.template: When {trigger} occurs, the AI will {action} and send the result to {output}.

questions.question: What's the first step in building any AI automation?
How many nodes minimum does a useful automation need?
options: At least 1 — even a single node can be useful

questions.question: What's the most valuable skill for an AI Operator?

steps.body:
TRIGGER: [Your trigger event]
ACTION: [What the AI should do]
OUTPUT: [Where results go]

This is your first workflow template. Copy it, fill it in, and you've designed your first automation.
Paste your filled-in workflow template here to lock in your first design. The AI will check it and give you feedback.

steps.question:
Compare these two approaches:

APPROACH A: Write a ChatGPT prompt every morning to summarize yesterday's support tickets.

APPROACH B: Set up an automation that pulls tickets at 8 AM, runs the summary, and posts it to Slack automatically.

Which approach is sustainable when you have 100+ tickets daily?
You've built an automation that categorizes incoming emails. It works 95% of the time. The other 5% of emails go to a 'review' folder. What should you do?
options: Scrap the automation — 5% error rate is too high
Review the 5% manually — the automation saved you 95% of the work
Keep tweaking until it's 100% perfect before using it
Add a second AI to review the 5%
feedback: Perfect answer. Don't let perfect be the enemy of good. 95% automation is still a massive win. Review the edge cases manually until you can refine the automation further.
Think again. Perfect is the enemy of shipped. 95% automation is excellent. Handle the exceptions manually and iterate over time.

steps.prompt:
Look at your work from the last week. Identify ONE task you do repeatedly that follows a clear pattern — something with consistent inputs, rules, and outputs.

Describe the task. What triggers it? What information do you need? What do you produce at the end?

questions.label: How confident are you in building your first automation?
What's the first repetitive task you'd like to automate with AI?

steps.title: What You Just Built
steps.body:
You didn't just learn about automation — you designed one. You identified a trigger, defined an action, set conditions, and specified an output. That's the same pattern every operator workflow follows, from simple email categorization to complex multi-step pipelines.

You've also experienced many of the platform's interaction types: scenario cards for context, multiple choice and true/false for knowledge checks, fill-in-the-blank and matching for precision, good-fit analysis for judgment, builder for creation, and copy/paste for real-world use.

Tomorrow: the complete operator's toolkit. You'll experience every remaining interaction type the platform offers — a preview of everything you'll use across the program.

steps.title: Day 2 Complete!
steps.body:
You just designed your first AI automation workflow. More importantly, you learned how to think like an operator — not someone who uses AI, but someone who builds with it.

Tomorrow: your complete toolkit. You'll experience every interaction type the platform offers — the full operator's workshop. After that, you'll start chaining these tools into real pipelines.
